data.title: Opportunity Knocks: Two Of My Favorite Dividend Stocks Are On Sale
data.description:
Introduction

Earnings season has arrived!

Just like in prior quarters, it's all about big tech again.

Going into this month, the ten biggest S&P 500 members accounted for roughly 36% of its weighting. This makes it somewhat obvious that all eyes will be on their earnings, as the capital of countless investors depended on it - at least to a certain extent.

Using the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), as a proxy, the median market cap of the S&P 500 is slightly more than $260 billion! I just checked my portfolio. Just three out of 24 holdings have a market cap higher than that.

This makes sense, as the index is dominated by FANG+ holdings and Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A). The biggest two holdings alone account for almost 14% of the S&P 500's total weighting.

In other words, because of their weighting, their earnings matter a lot.

However, there's more to it. Their earnings also matter because the biggest ten stocks of the S&P 500 are responsible for more than 40% of S&P 500 research and development expenses and roughly a quarter of the index's free cash flow and operating income.

Moreover, as reported by The Wall Street Journal, capital spending is expected to rise to more than $60 billion this quarter, up almost 60% compared to last year.

Thus, Wall Street expects their combined capital bill to top $60 billion in the quarter ended September, up 56% year over year, according to consensus estimates from Visible Alpha. Another sizable double-digit jump is expected for the December quarter, which would bring the year’s total outlay to about $231 billion, some 49% higher than 2023. - The Wall Street Journal

In other words, the "big guys" are also expected to serve as an indicator of demand for other companies. For example, if AI-related spending remains strong, it bodes well for companies engaged in data center production, semiconductor operations, and so much more.

The only problem is that the bar is very high. According to FactSet, the number of S&P 500 technology companies issuing positive EPS guidance has risen to the highest level since 3Q21.

Using the chart below, we clearly see that one sector is not like the others.

With that said, while everyone is focused on big tech, I'm focusing on other areas.

As we can see in the chart below, the "Mag 7" is expected to see 18% EPS growth. The remaining 493 stocks are expected to see a 0.4% EPS contraction. This is one of the reasons why the rotation has been very slow, as the market had just too many reasons to stick with the Mag 7 instead of rotating to other opportunities.

However, after 3Q24, S&P 493 EPS growth is expected to accelerate, potentially maintaining double-digit EPS growth through at least 4Q25.

With that said, this earnings season, I'm focused on smaller companies with important business models that I expect to beat the market over time.

In this article, I'll present two of my favorite dividend (growth) stocks.

  • One has a somewhat low yield but consistent dividend growth and aggressive buybacks.
  • One has a decent yield and elevated dividend growth.
  • Both have business models that are critical to the supply chains they serve.
  • Both have fantastic valuations.
  • Both sold off after earnings.

To be honest, I often prefer to research the companies that sell off after earnings, as I have found a lot of gems this way over the past few years.

So, let's start with the wide-moat pick that comes with consistent dividend growth, aggressive buybacks, and a wide-moat business model.

CSX Corp. (CSX) - If I Didn't Have So Much Railroad Exposure Already...

My most recent in-depth research -> (Article)

CSX is America's second-largest public Class I railroad. Together with its peer Norfolk Southern (NSC), it has a duopoly on railroad transportation in the East of the United States.

This business model comes with a wide-moat advantage, as it is almost impossible for new entrants to build a similar business model. Moreover, more than 75% of its revenues come from high-margin bulk and merchandise operations that have limited competition from over-the-road transportation.

As I have written in prior articles, the only reason why I do not own CSX is that I already own three other railroads.

I own Union Pacific (UNP), Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP), and Norfolk Southern. These three investments cover all three North American nations, including intermodal, merchandise, and a lot of bulk.

The company just reported its 3Q23 earnings. Although I liked the numbers, it sold off, as economic uncertainty is expected to last. That's great news, as I really like the value CSX shares offer at these levels.

With that said, in its just-released earnings, the company reported strong numbers, especially considering weakness in cyclical sectors (like manufacturing), which are major drivers of Class I railroad volumes.

Using the data below, we see that 3Q24 revenue was up 1%, supported by 6% higher merchandise revenues and 3% higher volumes. Operating margins rose by 180 basis points to 37.4%, implying a 62.6% operating ratio (100-37.4).

Moreover, operating income grew by 7%, benefitting from effective cost management. Earnings per share rose by 12%, benefiting from margin improvements and the company's commitment to return capital through buybacks. After all, buying back stock improves the per-share value of the business.

Buybacks are visible at the very bottom of the table below.

Over the past ten years, CSX has bought back roughly 35% of its shares, making it one of the most aggressive buyers of its own shares. Year-to-date, the Florida-based company has spent $1.2 billion on buybacks. This is down from $2.9 billion in the prior-year period.

Below, we clearly see that CSX is reducing buybacks. Year-to-date, it has spent $1.9 billion on buybacks and dividends, 86% of its free cash flow. Last year, shareholder distributions exceeded free cash flow.

Moreover, while CSX yields just 1.4%, the dividend comes with a five-year CAGR of 8.5% and almost 20 conservative annual hikes. Looking at its dividend history below, we see that the focus on precision railroading and industry consolidation has turned it into a very consistent dividend grower.

While I cannot make the case that CSX is a great pick for income-focused investors, it has one of the best total return profiles. This allowed it to slightly beat the S&P 500 over the past ten years, despite struggling with weakening manufacturing growth since 2022.

Going forward, I expect CSX to beat the market as well, supported by economic re-shoring, improvements in its network fluidity, and higher energy prices supporting (international) coal demand.

Its 6% growth rate in 3Q24 merchandise revenue was a good start, as it benefitted from new business wins, favorable truck conversions, and new industrial development projects.

As we can see below, the East is home to many economic mega projects, expected to provide CSX with higher demand on a longer-term basis.

The only problem is that, for now, the market cares more about short-term developments. As the company sees lower revenue and limited margin improvements in 4Q24 due to hurricanes, it makes sense that CSX shares are softer after earnings.

If I had more cash and less railroad exposure, I would be a buyer at these levels, as CSX remains one of the best dividend growth stocks on my radar.

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, analysts expect EPS to remain flat this year. This is down from 4% growth when I wrote my most recent article. I agree with this downgrade, as 4Q24 challenges make it close to impossible to grow EPS.

However, after 2024, analysts expect double-digit EPS growth in both 2025 and 2026. As CSX now trades at a blended P/E ratio of 17.9x, a point below its ten-year average of 18.8x, it is in a good spot to return more than 13% per year going forward.

Needless to say, I'll stick to a Buy rating, believing CSX is one of the best "earnings victims" to buy on weakness.

The same goes for the next pick, a stock with a higher yield.

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) - Another Great Post-Earnings Buying Opportunity

My most recent in-depth research -> (Article)

I wasn't planning on writing another article about Rexford, as I have covered it in a number of articles in the past few months. However, because the company sold off after reporting earnings on October 16, I decided to cover it again, as I really like the risk/reward at these levels. I also like that its much larger peer, Prologis (PLD), came out making the case for bottoming demand in the industrial real estate sector.

The world’s largest warehouse owner is sending upbeat signals about the direction of the U.S. logistics market. Industrial-property heavyweight Prologis boosted its profit about 25% last quarter and revenue also increased, defying a broader downturn in the market for logistics facilities.

[...] Prologis says that with distribution centers now filling up and construction of new sites waning, the pieces are in place for a rebound. Like J.B. Hunt, the company sees a cycle on the cusp of turning around. - The Wall Street Journal (Logistics Report)

However, unlike Prologis, Rexford solely invests in Southern California's ("SoCal") infill market. In this market, it has 424 properties that serve more than 1,600 customers. Prologis generates roughly a fifth of its revenue in this market, as it is one of the biggest industrial markets in the world.

SoCal is home to two of the biggest ports in the world (Los Angeles and Long Beach), good colleges, one of the biggest consumer bases, and the fourth-largest industrial market on the planet.

Although SoCal is prone to political risks, it is still the gateway to the western two-thirds of the United States and a market that is wedged between mountains and the ocean. This provides it with supply tailwinds, as it is very hard to build new industrial buildings in this region.

When adding an increasing focus on residential real estate through conversion, I have to say that SoCal industrial real estate (especially warehouses) is a great place to be.

With that said, just like CSX, the stock sold off after earnings, which is why I am even more interested in the company. In the case of REXR, I believe selling was caused by mixed guidance.

While guidance was far from poor, we see that the company made unfavorable adjustments to its same-property net operating income growth expectations and its full-year occupancy.

At the end of the third quarter, it had a 97.6% occupancy rate. Although the company expects some weakness on a full-year basis, it continues to enjoy strong tailwinds in SoCal, which somewhat protect it against nationwide headwinds from poor consumer sentiment, weakening manufacturing sentiment, and rising vacancy rates after a wave of new construction projects hit the market after the pandemic.

Looking beyond these issues, we can be upbeat, as we can expect tailwinds from both demand and supply. This is what Cushman & Wakefield wrote in their latest 3Q24 industrial real estate report (emphasis added):

  • With new supply fading and demand poised to rebound next year, we expect vacancy to peak at 6.7% midway through 2025 (still below the historical average) and then trend lower.
  • After this year, and with many major occupier consolidations behind us, we anticipate the engines driving demand for industrial space—e-commerce, 3PL, consumer spending, housing recovery and onshoring—to kick into a higher gear. Net absorption is forecast to increase from around 100 msf in 2024 to over 200 msf in 2025, and more than 260 msf in 2026.

This bodes well for REXR. Even without an expected recovery, the company estimates it has roughly 34% cash net operating income growth embedded in its existing portfolio. This assumption is based on zero market rent growth, with most growth coming from repositioning/redevelopment and mark-to-market adjustments.

In other words, these are proceeds from asset improvements and rent adjustments to market rents.

Speaking of rents, the company also enjoys strong leasing spreads, including 39% on a net effective basis and 27% on a cash basis. Moreover, annual embedded rent steps within executed leases averaged 3.9%.

The bad news is that market rents are still under pressure. Until the aforementioned demand and supply tailwinds kick in, we'll likely continue to see market rent normalization.

So far, however, REXR is protected, as it has more than $70 million in mark-to-market opportunities, as the overview above shows.

Leasing spreads in the quarter showed continued strength at 39% and 27% on a net effective and cash basis, respectively, in line with our prior quarter projections. Additionally, annual embedded rent steps in our executed leases averaged 3.9%. Excluding the lease up of the 275,000 square foot DuPont repositioning project, rent steps averaged 4%, in line with prior quarters year-to-date. - REXR 3Q24 Earnings Call

This also explains why the company isn't worried when it deals with the bankruptcy of LL Flooring. This company rents a 504,000 sq ft property. Although losing LL Flooring is far from great, the company used to rent the property at rates way below the market rate. REXR will redevelop the property and potentially turn this headwind into a tailwind.

Moreover, while current pressure on rents has caused some investors to sell REXR, we see that new developments in SoCal are also way down. This makes it very likely that this market will emerge as one of the biggest winners next year.

I also need to add that REXR has a market share of just 2.7% in SoCal. As most of the buildings are mom-and-pop-owned assets, there is a lot of room for long-term expansion and redevelopment.

Then there's REXR's dividend. Currently yielding 3.7%, REXR has hiked its dividend by 18% per year over the past five years. Its most recent hike was 10% on February 5.

Valuation-wise, REXR trades at a blended P/AFFO (adjusted funds from operations) ratio of 23.8x. Although this may seem like a very lofty valuation, analysts expect the company to grow per-share AFFO by 12% this year, potentially followed by 13% and 14% growth in 2025 and 2026, respectively.

Applying a 25x multiple implies a fair stock price of $62, 38% above the current price.

In other words, I actually like that REXR sold off after earnings, as I am considering buying it for my own portfolio and a number of family accounts.

The only reason I do not own it is because I simply lack the funds to add a new major position. I added four new investments this year and have to increase my war chest again. This excludes smaller investments and dividend reinvestments.

Takeaway

Earnings season always puts the spotlight on big tech, but I'm looking in other places for opportunities. While the "Mag 7" stocks dominate headlines, I'm focused on companies that may have sold off post-earnings but still offer strong fundamentals.

CSX, with its consistent dividend growth and buybacks, and Rexford Industrial Realty, a key player in Southern California industrial real estate, both stand out as great buys on weakness.

Their long-term opportunities, driven by impressive business models and market positioning, make them attractive despite recent volatility.

If I had more cash, these would be some of the top contenders for my portfolio right now.

data.title: SKYX Platforms Is Firing Up The Cylinders
data.description:
Skyx Platforms (NASDAQ:SKYX) offers a range of smart home solutions in lightening and ceiling fans and a smart-home platform.

The company offers a patented universal power plug and receptacle that is easier and safer to install (no need for handling wires):

The company is increasingly adding smarts to the products to become a smart home and building producer and platform with products like:

  • Universal Power-Plug & Receptacle enabling plug-and-play installation of light fixtures and ceiling fans, eliminating the need to handle hazardous wires.
  • Smart Universal Power-Plug & Receptacle adding smart features like app and voice control, scheduling, and energy-saving modes to the same plug-and-play installation.
  • Smart Plug and Play Lighting and Ceiling Fans combine plug-and-play installation with smart technology controlled through the SkyHome App.
  • Smart Sky Platform, an all-in-one smart home platform that integrates various safety and smart home features, including smoke and carbon monoxide detectors, sensors, and more.

The company is going through three generations of products, all of which offer big opportunities:

The company will advance from selling products to becoming a platform with its smart home and buildings platform:

Business model

There are various planks in the business model:

  • Creating a new safe electrical ceiling standard and fostering its adoption.
  • This is a razor and blade model as an increasing variety of products fit its plug-and-play safe ceiling standard.
  • Adding smart home features and creating a platform also with a razor & blade model (see below under Smart Sky Platform).
  • The company sells through some 60+ different e-commerce sites
  • The company is partnering with the likes of Home Depot, GE Licensing, Ruee Appliances, Kichler, Quoizel and Golden Lightning.
  • The company licenses its technology to extent its reach and increase the chance of becoming the standard.

A New Standard?

SKYX has filed an application with the NEC seeking mandatory safety standardization for its ceiling outlet receptacle platform. Needless to say, if this is approved, it would significantly boost sales by requiring the use of SKYX's technology in new homes and buildings.

While management is confident they have everything that's needed to become mandatory, there is no guarantee of approval or any set timeframe for a decision. There is progress:

  • SKYX's weight-bearing safe plug-and-play outlet/receptacle has already received NEC inclusion and is represented by ten different segments in the NEC Code Book.
  • The NEC has adopted a change to the definition of "receptacle" based on SKYX's technology, which management considers a significant development.
  • SKYX has received NEC generic name approval for its weight-bearing safe plug-and-play outlet/receptacle for ceilings (WSCR) and its ceiling plug (WSAF).
  • This standardization approval from ANSI and NEMA signifies recognition and acceptance of SKYX's technology by leading U.S. standardization organizations.

We're still way off its plug becoming a mandatory safety standard (if that indeed happens) and this depends on regulatory bodies, but there are things the company can do to increase its chances and that is for the plug to become as ubiquitous as possible (basically becoming the de-facto standard).

This is where licensing and partnering come in, which accelerate market acceptance.

Growth drivers

There are multiple growth drivers, many of which are arriving at once:

  • New customers
  • New products
  • New partners and channels
  • Licensing

New customers

There is plenty of evidence this is working as its products are in 10K+ US and Canadian homes and management expects this to be in the tens of thousands next year.

We expect to significantly enhance our market penetration to the builder markets in the coming months.

Partners

Key partners are:

  • EGLO, a European World Leading Design and Manufacturer of Decorative Lighting and Ceiling Fans.
  • Home Depot, sells a host of its products through Home Depot's 2K+ stores and website.
  • GE Licensing, a five-year licensing partnership agreement for the U.S. and global markets, established SKYX's ceiling outlet/receptacle technology as the standard for homes and buildings.
  • Ruee Appliances, the biggest lighting manufacturer in the world.
  • Kichler, entering a product collaboration with high-end lighting and ceiling fan company Kichler
  • Quoizel, a U.S. leading lighting company and manufacturer.
  • Golden Lightning, a U.S. leading elegant lighting company and manufacturer.

Here is what the 5-year deal (with automatic one-year renewals) entails, from the linked PR:

GE's licensing team will provide licensing services, including seeking and arranging for licensee partners in the U.S. and globally, negotiating agreement terms, administering contracts, auditing partners, assisting with monetization, and patent protection strategy, while providing mutually agreed support to defend the Company's intellectual property.

GE will get a percentage of the revenues generated through these efforts.

The deal with Ruee involves Ruee providing several services in areas like financial, mass production, manufacturing capabilities, and distribution to the global markets.

Not only does Ruee extend the company reach, it also provides crucial financial leeway which management has likened to the Dell Working Capital Model (Q2CC):

Dell Computer really revolutionized the direct sales or rapid sales to customers and they were able to collect their receivables within one to three days. And they were able to have favorable vendor payables that could be over 10 times that time span. So, it could be 30 to 60 days. And that significantly enhances the cash conversion cycle and the liquidity generated from working capital.

Very important as the company is still quite some way off from producing positive cash flow.

New products

SKYX plans to introduce a wider variety of products, including wall sconces, ceiling fans, flush mounts, downlights, exit signs, and emergency lights. This will allow them to target the builder market more effectively. The partnership with Ruee is enabling this product expansion (Q2CC):

part of our relationship with collaborating with those vendors, manufacturers, including Ruee, is to address the variety of products. Currently, our revenues mainly go our product and plug and play, go on smart chandeliers. Plug and play chandeliers and pendants. But now we're opening the door for recessed light, wall sconces, ceiling fans, flush mounts, down lines, what we call and down the road exit signs and emergency lights. Those are definitely key to builders.

Then there is the recently introduced, patented Recessed Light (Q2CC):

We announced this Recessed Light, some people call it hi-hats. No patent on plug and play people still, it's a small, very small fixture, but people still go on ladder, spend time on the ladder, touching hazardous wires and taking can take 30 minutes to someone install one of them. Here it becomes plug and play. This is -- as we said in our press release and earlier, this is a multibillion-unit market. We're very excited that we started production. Those are part of things that are going to go on The Home Depot. This potential product opens the door for many builders and other projects that we're talking to now and we hope we'll be able to announce in the next coming months.

This has also great potential globally, not just in the US.

Smart Home Platform

Then there is the Gen 3 All-in-One Smart Sky Platform which begins mass production late this year. The platform integrates smart and safety features into a single device, simplifying smart home installation and enhancing performance.

This can transform houses and buildings into smart ones in minutes, rather than weeks.

The company won multiple awards with this platform at CES in January. There are a host of possible subscription services like:

  • Communications like internet services.
  • Fire Alarms; leveraging the platform's integrated smart smoke detector.
  • Home Intrusion Alerts through utilizing sensors and connectivity features within the platform.
  • Emergency Response Services, involving partnerships with emergency response providers or integration with existing emergency services.
  • Monitoring Services, using the platform's various sensors to monitor aspects like movement, smoke, fire, carbon monoxide, temperature, humidity, and other environmental conditions and hazards. The platform could also potentially monitor home access and visitors.

The platform can be offered to a host of different markets and segments, like homeowners, rental property owners/managers, hotels, cruise ships, elder living facilities, schools, hospitals, offices, commercial buildings, and retail businesses.

These are all plans and opportunities, it remains to be seen how fast these can materialize.

Licensing

SKYX intends to generate revenue through licensing its patented technologies. They are currently focused on licensing its standard and smart plug-and-play technologies to various industries, including tech, smart home, AI, lighting, ceiling fans, and electrical,

This strategy allows SKYX to capitalize on its intellectual property and generate revenue without having to manufacture and sell products directly, as well as widens its reach and increases the chance of becoming standard.

The deal with GE licensing in particular is a significant step forward here.

International

The company is already generating some sales from Canada but overseas markets constitute a huge opportunity. Recent deals with partners, especially the ones with Ruee and EGLO give it significant international exposure so we are keen to see how this develops.

Finances

  • Revenue was $21.4M in Q2, up from $15M in Q2/23 and up 13% sequentially.
  • Gross margin increased 400bp sequentially to 30.7%.
  • OpEx decreased 11% sequentially to $12.8M and cash OpEx decreased 14% to $8.7M.
  • Adj EBITDA loss was $2.1M versus $4.6M in Q2/23.

So we see revenue growth, gross margin expansion, and a decline in dollar terms in OpEx, all conspiring to improve financials even if the company isn't cash flow positive yet:

The company had $15.6M of cash left, that's $4.2M less than at the end of Q1. Management argues that the company will become cash flow positive sometime next year and the Dell-like financing that Ruee enables them to be frugal with cash gives them a good shot at avoiding additional financing needs until then.

Valuation

Given the negative cash flow dilution has been significant:

And there is a lot more on the way, from the 10-Q:

So fully diluted there are 146.5M shares, which at $1.2 per share produces a market cap of $175.8M and an EV of $160.2M. The shares are selling below 1.7x FY24 EV/S and 1.2x FY25 EV/S. We're inclined to say that's fairly cheap.

Conclusion

The company has many things going for it:

  • Unique technology and solutions that fill a real need and have a huge TAM.
  • Multiple growth drivers seem to arrive all at once, like a host of new partners opening significant new channels like Home Depot, new segments and opportunities abroad, and new products, especially the recessed light and the smart home platform which opens up the possibility of recurring revenue streams, licensing deals with the likes of GE,
  • Any single one of these, like a single product line sold through Home Depot, could improve revenue materially, together these developments could open the floodgates of demand.
  • Management is clearly expecting this as they see their products in tens of thousands of homes next year versus 10K homes today.
  • A bit of a wild card, but its ceiling outlet receptacle platform could become a mandatory safety standard, which would be like hitting the jackpot.
  • While the company is still bleeding significant amounts of cash, partners like Ruee are very helpful here greatly reducing the company's cash needs. Management thinks they have enough cash to generate their own, somewhere next year and they could be right.
  • The shares are not expensive.

The risk is a slowdown in growth on a slowing economy scuppering management expectations of becoming cash flow positive next year and needing additional financing. Given the growth vectors that are lined up, this seems a small but non-negligible risk to us.

data.title: The Momentum Weekly - Interesting Week As The Market Preps For Earnings Season
data.description:
Introduction

On balance, the intermediate term generally looks like we are tiptoeing towards "trending" as discussed in last week's Momentum Weekly, but shorter term the market seems like it could use some pullback or rest. Given this, I am managing stops and cautious with adding new risk until the market really declares itself, especially with the presidential election, earnings season, and geopolitics all ongoing. Software stocks are continuing to set up very nicely, and story stocks (aka speculative trash) are having their moment in the sun.

Market Analysis

Nasdaq (QQQ)

Despite the index grinding out an unremarkable +0.24% this week, it was pretty interesting under the hood.

I see a lot of similarity to the Higher High from a few weeks ago (red box). Big red distribution day (fingerpoint down), followed by a very uninspiring rally day, another red day (this Thursday’s being of the gap and crap variety) followed by another uninspiring rally day. I also don’t exactly love the fact that the market shrugged off great earnings from Netflix (NFLX) and Taiwan Semiconductors (TSM) – two liquid, important, mega cap stocks – and couldn’t even get a glimpse at Tuesday’s highs. On the upside, the market found buyers at the 10 EMA on both Tuesday and Wednesday before putting some air to it today.

The market seems a little tired here as it flirts with all time highs, and it wouldn’t shock me to see a pullback to put in another Higher Low. I continue to think and “feel” that we are heading towards another period of “trending” in the intermediate timeframe, but in the 1-2 week timeframe, chop and pullback would be fine. If we really roll over, however, breaking the pattern of Higher Lows, it would be back to the drawing board for me. I have it drawn up like I am hoping to see it in the chart below, but, as always, I will respond to price,NOT to my hopes or expectations.

When you couple this short term outlook with earnings season volatility, presidential election volatility, and the possibility of geopolitical volatility from the Middle East, I plan to be cautious here. I will be very selective with new positions, open them with tight stops, trust my stops on my current positions, and wait for the market to give me clear confirmation that it is ready to start “trending” before getting aggressive.

Small Caps (IWM)

Taking some earned chop for a few days after a robust push out of this consolidation. Not quite eclipsing the highs from July's failed rally and would not like to see it completely fall apart as it approaches that level. More chop would be healthy and not at all surprising while the EMAs catch up to price.

Gold (GLD)

Gold looks beautiful. Could have added more with the recent mini base breakout on Wednesday, but I chose to just trail what I have. Hoping for a long term hold on this one.

Bitcoin (BTC)

Bitcoin looks like it is trying to break out of this enormous flag pattern, although it has hard to say for sure, as it has certainly put in a number of takeouts since the highs in March. I also note that the character seems to have changed a bit, putting in a somewhat cleaner pattern of higher highs, higher lows, and consolidations.

Daily chart on the left, weekly on the right. Would like to see this form a bit more of a handle as we definitively push to break out of this flag.

Bitcoin related names like Microstrategy (MSTR) and Coinbase (COIN) also seem to be telling us that this time is for real. I am currently holding Core Scientific (CORZ) from the breakout last Friday.

Although I only trade charts in my trading accounts, my fundamental bias will inevitably bleed over: I continue to believe in the long term Bitcoin story. Long term capital flows from retail/retirement accounts to Bitcoin ETFs and delayed effects from the Halving could push prices over time. Some say the Halving phenomenon is dead, but I do not agree. This year’s Halving was overhyped and priced in way ahead of time and thus didn’t move the needle at the actual time. However, as this affects miners over the long run, this should impact supply/demand dynamics.

If Bitcoin really is for real this time, I wouldn’t be shocked to see it break this flag in a big way over the next 6-12 months. The volatility will, no doubt, be crazy, but a six figure handle would not surprise me.

I will primarily be watching MSTR, COIN, and CORZ as ways to play it, as they have the best looking charts in the bitcoin space. iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) is another option, but I generally prefer trading real Bitcoin to the ETF wrappers.

Portfolio

Some thoughts on a few portfolio positions.

As a note, I try to tell you what I am planning to do and why in case it is informative for anyone in their own decision making, but it is on the reader to make decisions appropriate to their risk tolerance and individual situation.

Trading is nothing more than a game of, on average and over the balance of many trades, selling stocks for more in gains than you stop out of the losers for... then recycling that edge over again and again.

If you want to learn more, the rabbit hole starts with How to Make Money in Stocks, which may open your eyes to an entirely new investing philosophy and could give you the foundation you need to start momentum trading.

Reddit (RDDT)

Love the action we have been seeing in Reddit as it breaks out to all time highs, but I trimmed pretty heavily into strength today. Earnings is around the corner, and we’ve had a good run the last few weeks. While it certainly does happen, you don’t often see stocks run really well and then rocket even further on earnings. A good run tends to price in a good earnings report, so earnings have to be REALLY good to get it going further. On the other hand, any disappointment has the potential to completely rock the stock.

Planning to hold most of what I currently have into earnings, unless it violates a stop.

NVIDIA (NVDA)

As I wrote last week, it is a really big deal that the market’s liquid leader is back in play. NVDA spent most of the week chopping around just under all time highs. It is a little stretched from the 10 and 21 EMAs, though, and Tuesday was a pretty robust distribution day (big red move with big red volume). Thus, it wouldn’t shock me to see this pull in and test the 10 or even 21 EMAs. Longer term, this would be healthy, setting us up to break out of this months long base. Just trending my stops and following the action.

Maplebear (CART)

This thing seems to be lacking in power after a great first move from my buy point (fingerpoint). I trimmed a bit on Tuesday to lock in a profit and on the break of the 10 EMA. It actually looked set for a continuation breakout today from that new range I drew, but it pulled a “gap and crap.”

The 21 EMA is now above my buypoint, and I have booked a small profit, so I am willing to see where this goes into next week.

Lumen Technologies (LUMN)

Not saying it's guaranteed to trade well from here, but I really liked this setup as noted in the Mid Week Update comments. I thought I would show it as a great illustration of what I am looking for.

Big, explosive move on the left side. Sideways for a handful of months to digest the gains before putting in a tight, descending channel right into the 50 SMA. Notice how much more orderly that channel on the right is compared to the one I drew in to the left. We then had three tight days that got tighter and tighter with the 50 SA underneath for support and the 10 and 21 EMAs stacked just overhead. Today it plowed through.

All the caveats about tight stops, risk management, and speculative trash apply.

Other Positions

GE Verona (GEV) – continues to trend, getting extended into earnings. Probably going to trim heavily for all the same reasons noted re Reddit above.

Palantir (PLTR) – continues to trend, great support action on the 10 EMA this week. Probably the leader of the software stocks. More on software below. Might be forming another flag-ish base right now, but I probably would not add with earnings around the corner.

Samsara (IOT) – I took a support buy on Wednesday as detailed in my Mid Week Update. Not convinced this was a great decision, but I have been looking closely at software. Not much power here. More on software below. Stop at Wednesday’s low but willing to let this chop out a bit in the meantime.

Coherent Corp (COHR) – took my 10 EMA stop today when it failed to reclaim after an analyst downgrade. To me, this is just noise. Trailing the rest on the 21 EMA. Probably going to chop in a range until earnings, and I’m planning to hold a decent piece through.

CAVA Group (CAVA) – sort of wedging up. Action is pretty uninspiring, but it’s not really giving me a reason to sell. Trimmed a bit today to lock in a small profit. Trending my stops at the 10 EMA; 21 EMA is right around my buy point.

Stop Outs

SharkNinja (SN) – lost the 10 and then 21 EMAs. Stopped out the full position for a solid if unspectacular ~15% gain.

Arista Networks (ANET) – I usually use staggered stops for closes below the 10 and 21 EMA but with a hard stop set at the point where I cannot watch any further profit erode. ANET ran that stop – a handful of dollars below the 21 EMA – on Tuesday. Naturally it has since traded back up. Hey, that’s trading.

Constellation Energy (CEG) - Tried this on Wednesday but cut half yesterday on the huge giveback and then the rest today around the close for a small loss. As noted, not really willing to pile on risk right now, and CEG is clearly having issues with the ~272.5 level. Remains on my watch list.

Watch List

Looking at my watch list, I’m noticing a lot of action in “themes” and “story stocks,” aka speculative trash. In this case, we are talking about SMR companies (Small Modular Reactors), Bitcoin names as discussed above, and space stocks. A lot of these are glorified science projects and, in a reasonable world, belong in the trash heap. I, of course, am not above trading them with good stops and good risk management.

One thing that gives me pause is that you often see story stocks get a bid later in the index’s move as cash rotates from stalwarts that need a breather. While the Nasdaq is not out to new all time highs, it is up ~20% from the latest bottom and showing some signs that it may need to take a breather as described above. Something to make note of.

GDS Holdings Ltd (GDS)

Front and center for me of the China plays. China stocks have consolidated pretty nicely over the last week or two, and GDS has a very clean supply demand level. Unlike some other China plays, it is not a gappy trader, and it is pretty liquid. I don’t love the "gap and crap" from today, so my preference would be to see another day or two of tight trading under the supply demand level, but this one looks more or less ready to me.

As a note, I trade China stocks smaller due to overnight headline risk, gap risk, and geopolitical risk.

Duolingo (DUOL)

Broke out of a multi month base by absolutely tearing up the right side. There was a buy point around 213 that would’ve been ideal when it finished digesting the earnings move in a continuation pattern.

It has started the process of consolidating gains again and without any meaningful give back, telling me there is demand for this thing. I would like to see this continue to chop for a bit while the 21 EMA and 50 SMA catch up to hopefully play it for a long, strong move. It would not shock me if this thing chopped right into earnings, and if it did so, an earnings gap up would be an attractive spot.

Zillow (Z)

This one toyed with me this week. I bought it on Tuesday as noted in my Mid Week Update, but it was lacking in power both on Tuesday and Wednesday, prompting me to trim before stopping me out Thursday. I am beating a dead horse when I keep saying that I like the story – decreasing interest rates means more home buying – and how much it has flown before… but I do! Right now this ~64.4ish level looks like it is setting up reasonably well. Hoping for a few more days right underneath it. Earnings is coming up, however, so I am keeping an eye on that.

Nuclear Power

Unfortunately I missed the uranium breakout as noted in my Mid Week Update, thinking the names were a touch extended. I also, unfortunately, didn’t see the Small Modular Reactor company breakout in time, although, looking back on the charts, I’m not convinced I would have bought in.

They sure have gone nuts, though, based on the news that Amazon is going to be investing in nuclear power ( Amazon, Google and Microsoft Are Investing in Nuclear Power ).

I’m hoping to see some of these names – NuScale Power (SMR), Oklo Inc (OKLO), Nano Nuclear Energy (NNE) (lagging the other two in this move) – set up a flag pattern over the next week or two, something I could actually buy.

Faster traders might be able to find more opportunity in shorter term swings. Buyer beware on these. Liquidity is good right now with the massive volume spike, but that could dry up quickly. These are more or less glorified science projects and will probably be super volatile as longs, shorts, and day traders pile in.

If you are going to play these without some meaningful consolidation, I’d probably play hourly charts. In fact, OKLO was pretty well set on the hourly chart coming into today as noted below and would have been good for a 25% or so at the top. Daily on the left, hourly on the right:

I personally did not play this, as I, frankly, suck at these, tending to overstay my welcome or getting too stressed out by the volatility. Trading isn’t just a matter of finding stocks that will go up: you also have to find stocks you can stomach holding on the way.

Software (IGV)

I highlighted IGV last week and just want to show it again. You can see it pushed out of this enormous base, and it looks like it’s starting to set a continuation pattern here. I am continuing to keep a close eye on some of the bigger software names that have been acting well: Fortinet (FTNT), Nutanix (NTNX), Palo Alto Networks (PANW), PLTR (a leader in this group, I am long), DataDog (DDOG), AppLovin (APP) (THE leader in this group), and IOT (long) are all trading well.

Space Stocks

More story stocks here. These are a group have been on the move this week. I am long Rocket Lab (RKLB) (small) from last Friday. LUNR, ASTS, and SPCE (laggard) are in the group as well and have also been active.

Pretty interested in seeing what ASTS does from here. I’d like to see it build out a bit of a handle below the ~30.6 supply/demand pivot. If it sets there nicely then punches out, I would be a buyer.

It was definitely buyable on Wednesday, which would have been ideal. So, if it doesn’t set up right for me under 30.6, I will be content to sit on the sideline. As a rule, if you miss the ideal buy point, you shouldn’t try to force a new one onto the chart, let the chart give you another great one, and if it doesn’t, move on with your life. Risk first.

Again, all the usual precautions – speculative trash, glorified science project, etc.

Conclusion

On balance, the intermediate term generally looks like we are tiptoeing towards "trending" as discussed in last week's Momentum Weekly, but shorter term the market seems like it could use some pullback or rest. Given this, I am managing stops and cautious with adding new risk until the market really declares itself, especially with the presidential election, earnings season, and geopolitics all ongoing. Software stocks are continuing to set up very nicely, and story stocks (aka speculative trash) are having their moment in the sun.

All charts courtesy of TradingView.

data.title: Rexford Industrial: Great Opportunity For Long-Term Investors To Buy The Dip
data.description:
This article was co-produced with Dividend Collectuh.

It pays to be a long-term investor — the more time you have in the market, usually the better off you'll be. That's because when stocks take a beating for whatever reason, you can buy the dip and bring down your cost basis. Like the saying goes, “time in the market beats timing the market.”

So, when my holdings offer me a nice opportunity by suffering a nice dip, I get euphoric as I'm able to pick up shares discounted to my average. This is currently happening with Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (NYSE:REXR), a stock I hold in my retirement account.

Mr. Market didn't seem too happy with the company's recent Q3 earnings. In this article, I discuss the company's latest earnings, headwinds, and why REXR may be a compelling buy for long-term investors.

Previous Strong Buy Thesis

I last covered Rexford Industrial this past July, right before the company reported its Q2 earnings. Analysts expected funds from operations, or FFO, of $0.58, in-line with the first quarter. I predicted FFO in a range of $0.59 — $0.61 due to the company's strong leasing activity during the quarter that saw them complete $1.1 billion worth of transactions across 3.2 million square feet.

Their FFO came right in the middle of my prediction at $0.60, beating estimates by $0.02 while revenue of $237.57 million also beat by $1.49 million. Due to the disappointment with their latest earnings, the stock is down over 7% at the time of writing, basically flat from July.

At the same time, the S&P has continued to perform well, up 5.20%.

Not As Bad As It Seems

As previously mentioned, Rexford Industrial reported their Q3 earnings, and the stock was down over $3 a share the day after. Maybe the market didn't like their FFO declining by a penny from the previous quarter?

Despite this, core FFO of $130 million grew double-digits from the previous year. Achieving 13.1% growth during tougher than normal times, especially for REITs due to high interest rates, is actually pretty impressive in my opinion.

But the Southern California market has been facing headwinds all year due to weaker supply and demand. And with REXR's occupancy dropping to 96.9% from 97.3% in the previous quarter, I think this is why their share price is experiencing volatility at the moment.

Year-over-year, this declined from 97.3%. Their largest peer Prologis (PLD) also saw their occupancy dip from the previous year. This stood at 96.2% during their latest earnings, a decline of 1.3% from Q3 '23. So, for comparison purposes, Rexford saw the smaller decline, a testament to the strength of the SoCal market.

However, the latter's property locations in Southern California is something that always seems to worry investors. For me, as a long-term investor, this is a perfect buying opportunity.

The REIT also saw double-digit consolidated portfolio NOI growth of 17.6% to $183.5 million.

Same-property NOI grew a solid 2.6% while cash NOI increased 5.3% but declined from Q3 '23 that saw same-property NOI increase 8.9% and 9.5% on a cash basis. This could also be a contributing factor for the share price decline.

During the quarter, REXR made two acquisitions and one disposition, achieving leasing spreads of 68% on a net effective basis and 49% on a cash-basis. Year-to-date, the REIT has made $1.4 billion in investments, compared to $1.2 billion in 2023.

So, from an operational standpoint, REXR performed well besides the slight drop in occupancy and lower NOI growth. Prologis saw a drop in both metrics as well, with NOI growth on a net effective and cash basis declining from 9.3% and 9.5% to 6.2% and 7.2% respectively. And while headwinds for the sector persist, the market overreacted to what, I thought, was a solid earnings report.
